content.text: The anchor element is the engine of the web. Every hyperlink, navigation menu, in-page jump, file download, and mailto reference is an
. Understanding its attributes — and knowing when Angular's
— is essential for building navigable, accessible, and secure web applications.

content.text: element (anchor) creates a hyperlink to another resource. When it carries an
attribute, it is interactive: the browser navigates when the user clicks it or presses Enter while it has keyboard focus. Without

becomes a placeholder link — it receives no tab stop and no interactive role. The element is inline by default and can contain text, images, or any phrasing content, but not other interactive elements (no nesting

Sometimes navigation must happen imperatively — after a form submission, after an API call, or in response to a user event that isn't a link click. Inject the
Screen readers announce link text to convey where the link leads. Generic link text (
) makes no sense when users navigate the link list in isolation. Write meaningful link text that describes the destination or action:

When a link opens in a new tab, inform users — either in the visible text or via
— so they are not surprised. A "(opens in new tab)" suffix is the conventional pattern.
Links should be at least 44×44 CSS pixels in tappable area (WCAG 2.5.5 / Apple HIG). Increase padding rather than font size if the text is small.

to look like a link, it still behaves like a button (no right-click menu, Space activates it). If you style an
to look like a button, it loses its link semantics and tab stop — don't do this.

A typical navigation bar combines routerLink for internal routes, routerLinkActive for styling the active route, and plain href for external links. The following standalone component shows a complete pattern.

// nav-bar.component.ts import { Component } from '@angular/core'; import { RouterLink, RouterLinkActive } from '@angular/router'; @Component({ selector: 'app-nav-bar', standalone: true, imports: [RouterLink, RouterLinkActive], template: `
Home Courses Profile Angular Docs
`, styles: [` nav { display: flex; gap: 1rem; } a { color: inherit; text-decoration: none; padding: 0.5rem; } a.active { border-bottom: 2px solid currentColor; font-weight: 600; } a:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid #3b82f6; outline-offset: 2px; } `] }) export class NavBarComponent {}

[routerLinkActiveOptions]="{ exact: true }" on the Home link prevents it from staying active on every route (since every route starts with /). External links always use href with target="_blank" and rel="noopener noreferrer".
Query parameters and fragments in depth

routerLink accepts queryParams and fragment inputs to build URLs with search strings and hash fragments. The queryParamsHandling option controls what happens to existing query parameters when navigating.
Beginner courses Sort newest Installation
merges new params with existing ones;
keeps existing params unchanged; empty string (default) replaces all params.
Download links and the download attribute
The download attribute on an anchor tells the browser to save the resource rather than navigate to it. The attribute value becomes the suggested filename.
Download Report Export as CSV Download generated file

The download attribute only works for same-origin URLs or data URIs due to browser security restrictions. Cross-origin downloads require the server to send the Content-Disposition: attachment response header instead.
Angular provides RouterTestingHarness (Angular 15+) for testing component navigation in unit tests without needing a real browser. Older approaches use RouterTestingModule.
When testing routerLink, always include RouterTestingModule (or provideRouter with routes) in the imports. Without the Router module, routerLink will throw because it cannot find the router.

A skip link is a visually hidden anchor positioned at the very top of the page. When a keyboard user presses Tab, the skip link appears and lets them jump past the navigation directly to the main content — saving many keystrokes on every page load.
